questions.question: What are 'continuing bonds' in the context of grief?

options.text: An unhealthy attachment that prevents you from moving forward
An ongoing, evolving relationship with the deceased that can be a healthy part of grieving
A therapeutic technique only used by grief counselors
Pretending the person is still alive to avoid pain

questions.explanation: Continuing bonds theory recognizes that many bereaved people maintain an ongoing, evolving relationship with the deceased — and that this can be a healthy, adaptive part of grieving. This means carrying the person with you in meaningful ways, not pretending they are still alive or refusing to acknowledge reality.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of continuing bonds?

options.text: Immediately removing all reminders of the person to move on
Refusing to acknowledge the loss has occurred
Carrying forward values the person modeled, or maintaining rituals you shared
Avoiding all contact with people who knew the deceased

questions.explanation: Forms of continuing bonds include internal conversations, memory keeping, meaningful objects, rituals and traditions, carrying forward values and legacy, acts of service, and letter writing. These are ways to maintain meaningful connection that honors the relationship.

questions.question: When are continuing bonds most beneficial, according to research?

options.text: When they prevent you from engaging in any new activities or relationships
When they provide comfort, are flexible, evolve over time, and support daily functioning
Only during the first year after the loss
When they feel compulsive and obligatory

questions.explanation: Research suggests continuing bonds are most beneficial when they provide comfort rather than increasing distress, are flexible, evolve over time, support functioning rather than replacing all other activities, and feel chosen rather than compulsive or obligatory.

questions.question: How do continuing bonds apply to non-death losses like the end of a relationship?

options.text: They do not apply — continuing bonds only work with death
You should pretend the relationship never happened
You can recognize what the relationship taught you and carry forward the growth from that time
You should maintain active contact with the person at all costs

questions.explanation: Continuing bonds apply to non-death losses too. For relationship endings, this can mean recognizing what the relationship taught you, carrying forward the growth that happened during that time, and allowing yourself to value the good parts without having to reconcile the whole.

questions.question: When might continuing bonds become a concern that warrants professional support?

options.text: Whenever you talk to the deceased person in your mind
When you keep any meaningful objects belonging to the person
When the bond prevents all new engagement, feels compulsive, or is a source of increasing distress
When other people think you should have moved on by now

questions.explanation: Continuing bonds may become concerning if they prevent someone from engaging in any new activities or relationships for many months, if the connection feels compulsive and interferes with basic functioning, if there is persistent denial that the loss occurred, or if the bond is a source of increasing distress rather than comfort.
